shortDefinition: A yearly charge some credit cards levy simply for holding the card, often waived if you spend above a certain amount during the year.
definition: An Annual Fee is a recurring yearly charge that some credit card issuers add to your statement for keeping the card active, regardless of how much you use it. It's usually billed once a year, either on the card's issue anniversary or at a fixed time set by the bank. Not every card has one — many are 'lifetime free', meaning no annual fee is ever charged.
whyItMatters: The annual fee is a real, guaranteed cost of owning a card, separate from any interest you might pay. It's important to compare a card's benefits (rewards, cashback, lounge access) against its annual fee to see if the card is actually worth it for your spending pattern. A card with a high annual fee only makes sense if you use its perks enough to offset that cost.
realLifeExamples: A card with a ₹1,000 + GST annual fee might waive it automatically if you spend ₹1,00,000 or more on the card in the previous year — so a person spending ₹9,000/month would comfortably cross that and pay nothing extra.
commonMistakes: Not checking whether the annual fee is waived on spending and then being surprised when it appears on the statement despite low card usage.
tips: Before applying, check the exact annual fee, whether it's waived on spending, and how many years of fee-free use lifetime-free cards genuinely offer.

faqs.question: Is an annual fee the same as a joining fee?
faqs.answer: No. A joining fee is a one-time charge when the card is first issued, while an annual fee is charged every year the card stays active.

faqs.question: Can I get an annual fee reversed if I don't use the card much?
faqs.answer: Some banks may waive it as a one-time goodwill gesture if you call customer care, but this isn't guaranteed — it depends on the issuer's policy and your relationship with the bank.
